title: Chemistry: Equations and Stoichiometry Made Clear
lead: Balancing equations and stoichiometry are where many chemistry students get stuck. The logic is consistent—once you see the pattern, it clicks.

sections.title: Balancing Equations: The Rules
content: You can't create or destroy atoms. Change coefficients, not subscripts. Start with elements that appear in one place on each side. Practice with simple equations until it becomes automatic.

sections.title: Stoichiometry: Ratios and Proportions
content: The coefficients in a balanced equation tell you the mole ratio. Use that to convert between amounts of reactants and products. Set up proportions and solve.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Forgetting to balance first. Mixing up grams and moles. Skipping units. Careful setup and dimensional analysis prevent most errors.

sections.title: Practice With Variety
content: Do limiting reactant problems, percent yield, and excess reactant problems. The same logic applies—the scenarios change, the approach doesn't.

cta.title: Master Chemistry
cta.text: GuruForU tutors can walk your child through equations and stoichiometry step by step.

faq.question: What are the rules for balancing chemical equations?
faq.answer: You can't create or destroy atoms. Change coefficients, not subscripts. Start with elements that appear in one place on each side. Practice until it becomes automatic.

faq.question: What is stoichiometry?
faq.answer: The coefficients in a balanced equation give you mole ratios. Use those ratios to convert between amounts of reactants and products using proportions.

faq.question: What are common mistakes in stoichiometry?
faq.answer: Forgetting to balance first, mixing up grams and moles, and skipping units. Careful setup and dimensional analysis prevent most errors.

faq.question: What types of stoichiometry problems should I practice?
faq.answer: Limiting reactant, percent yield, and excess reactant problems. The logic is the same—only the scenarios change.
